line: He spent the whole breakfast explaining why he doesn't tip.
That's the opening statement.
Slow walk. Little Green Bag by George Baker Selection, 1969.
Tarantino licensed it for $5,000.
He knew exactly what it would do.

eiri.line: The film has no heist scene. The robbery happens entirely off-screen. Everything we see is before and after.

line: Mr. Orange is lying in the blood.
He stays there for the whole film.
Everything else happens around him.
The warehouse is a real location — a mortuary in Los Angeles.
Tarantino didn't tell the actors until they arrived.
Stuck in the Middle with You. Stealers Wheel, 1972.
Gerry Rafferty wrote it as a parody of Dylan.
After this scene it became something else entirely.

eiri.line: Michael Madsen improvised most of the dance. He said he just felt like moving.

line: The ear is never shown.
Tarantino cuts away at exactly the right moment.
What you imagine is worse than anything he could have filmed.
Every man has a color alias. Nobody uses their real name.
Mr. White knows Mr. Orange's real name.
That's the whole problem.

eiri.line: Harvey Keitel produced the film and played Mr. White. He came on board after reading the first scene — the Madonna speech.

line: Like a Virgin is about a girl who felt like a virgin again after being with a well-endowed man.
Joe says she's talking about a heartfelt feeling.
They're both right. That's the point.
The Mexican standoff has three guns pointing in three directions.
Nobody can fire without dying.
Tarantino would come back to this shape again and again.

eiri.line: Tim Roth rehearsed the undercover cop story for a month before filming. He performed it for the crew as a kind of audition. Tarantino then shot it three ways.

line: Mr. Orange says his name.
He's been bleeding the whole film to protect a lie.
He tells the truth at the moment it no longer matters.
The ending happens before we see it.
Mr. Pink is still running when the film cuts out.
We never find out if he made it.
